source_title: #398 Steve Jobs In His Own Words (Make Something Wonderful)

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: The best way to understand Steve is to listen to what he said and wrote over the course of his life. His words in speeches, interviews, and emails offer a window into how he thought, and he was an exquisite thinker. This is Make Something Wonderful, Steve Jobs in His Own Words, available for free from the Steve Jobs Archive. Laureen Powell Jobs wrote the introduction, and one of the most beautifully written ones I've read. It hits you when you think about it—that was her husband, the father of her children, somebody she was building her life with, and to have him gone so early is absolutely devastating.

paragraphs.speaker: steve
paragraphs.name: Steve Jobs
paragraphs.text: There's a lot of ways to be as a person. And some people express their deep appreciation in different ways. But one of the ways that I believe people express their appreciation to the rest of humanity is to make something wonderful and put it out there. And you never meet the people. You never shake their hands. You never hear their story or tell yours. But somehow, in the act of making something with a great deal of care and love, something's transmitted there. And it is a way of expressing to the rest of our species our deep appreciation.

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: Steve was constantly placing the products he was making in historical perspective. You can't do that unless you studied and learned from history. Edwin Land's idea—that you should build a company at the intersection of arts and technology—is an idea Steve learned in his early 20s and used for the rest of his life. If Steve was here today, he would tell you to study Edwin Land. That's why I repeat it so much. Ideas are immortal. Edwin Land passed away, Steve Jobs carried that torch, and now we're going to hopefully preach that lesson to people.

paragraphs.speaker: steve
paragraphs.name: Steve Jobs
paragraphs.text: I had a father who was a remarkable man. He never graduated from high school. He was a machinist by trade and worked very hard and was a kind of genius with his hands. He had a workbench out in the garage. When I was about five or six, he sectioned off a little piece of it and said, Steve, this is your workbench now. And he gave me some of his smaller tools and showed me how to use a hammer and a saw and how to build things. It was really very good for me. He spent a lot of time with me teaching me how to build things, how to take things apart and put them back together.

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: Steve's early love for reading is ever-present in history's greatest entrepreneurs. His mother taught him how to read before he got to school. And so when he got there, all he really wanted to do was just two things: read books because he loved reading books, and go outside and chase butterflies. He encountered authority of a different kind than he had ever encountered before and he did not like it. They really almost got me, he said. They came this close to really beating any curiosity out of me.

paragraphs.speaker: steve
paragraphs.name: Steve Jobs
paragraphs.text: After a few attempts, we managed to put together something that was the Apple one. All of our friends wanted them too. It turned out that it took maybe 50 hours to build one of these things by hand. We thought if we could get what's called a printed circuit board, we could just plug in the parts instead of having to hand wire the whole thing. Woz sold his HP calculator, which were very expensive things at the time. And I sold my VW bus. We took our idea for the computer to a few companies. One was HP, where Waz was working, and one was Atari, where Steve was working. No one was interested in pursuing it. So we started our own company.

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: This is what a trillion dollar company looks like 50 years before it's a trillion dollar company. No one else wanted to do this. So guess what? I guess we'll have to do it ourselves. And it just starts with two hobbyists building something that they want, them and their friends want to use, and they're willing to fund it by selling their car and their calculator.
